rationale: Anti-gaming: A misapplies the spec-114 escape. That escape is for CAN'T-fix-in-binary residuals (raw git merge, outside git's reach). Round 5 proved only the PROBE-level fix impossible; B is not a probe fix but a detection-SOURCE realignment, which IS an in-binary fix. Invoking 'can't fix in binary' to bless A when B exists is the gaming move. Worse, A's residual is NOT honestly complete: Fact 2 asserts a masked ref 'has no worktree or fails MergeInto', but in the transient-then-recover case the branch has a worktree AND MergeInto succeeds (transient cleared) — so Fact 2 is FALSE for the finding, and A ships either an overclaim ('fully closes') or an explicit admission of a silent un-gated merge. Both violate Max's property. B closes the identified TOCTOU by construction: the finding's vector is a refs/heads lock masking BranchExists (rev-parse --verify, gitops.go:98). WorktreeOps.List (bd worktree list, executor:44) does NOT probe that ref, so gate and merge read one source and cannot desync on it; ancestry stays fail-closed; FinalizeEpic itself already skips merge on ancErr (:395). No lost detection — Fact 2 confirms a worktreeless orphan is not mergeable, so the gate needn't see it. Residual under B: only a thin second-List admin-read transient, a narrower/different mechanism symmetric with FinalizeEpic's own List — disclose it honestly, do NOT claim absolute closure. B is gate-scoped, leaves the shared predicate untouched, and measures the real merge input rather than a ref-probe proxy — cleaner, not just safer.
rejected: A leaves the identified silent un-gated merge open, rests on a Fact-2 refutation that is false for transient-recover, and misuses the can't-fix-in-binary escape when B is the in-binary fix.
